steps.prompt: Which words are the finite verbs?

hints.venit only: There are two clauses joined by 'et non' — 'odit' is also a finite verb
hints.odit only: 'Venit' is also a finite verb meaning 'comes' — both are verbs
hints.quis and patrem: 'Quis' is an indefinite pronoun and 'patrem' is a noun — neither is a verb

hints.to sell: 'To sell' is 'vendere' — 'venit' here is from 'venire' (to come)
hints.to hunt: 'To hunt' is 'venari' — a deponent verb, not 'venire'
hints.to find: 'To find' is 'invenire' — 'venire' by itself means 'to come'

hints.perfect indicative: 'Venit' can be perfect (vēnit with long e), but in this conditional context it is present (vĕnit)
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'veniat' — 'venit' ends in -it (indicative)

hints.veneo, venire, venii, venitum: These are the parts of 'venire' meaning 'to be sold' — a different verb
hints.vendo, vendere, vendidi, venditum: These are the parts of 'vendere' (to sell)
hints.venor, venari, venatus sum, —: These are the parts of 'venari' (to hunt) — a deponent verb

hints.to love: 'To love' is 'amare' — the opposite of 'odisse' (to hate)

steps.prompt: What is unusual about the verb 'odit'?
steps.target: it is a defective verb — perfect in form, present in meaning
choices: it is a defective verb — perfect in form, present in meaning
steps.reveal: odit = he/she hates (perfect form, present meaning)

hints.it is a deponent verb: Deponents are passive in form, active in meaning — 'odit' is active in form but perfect-present
hints.it is an impersonal verb: Impersonal verbs lack a personal subject — 'odit' has 'quis' as subject
hints.it is a semi-deponent verb: Semi-deponents have present active and perfect passive — 'odi' has only perfect-system forms with present meaning

hints.audio, audire, audivi, auditum: These are the parts of 'audire' (to hear)
hints.oleo, olere, olui, —: These are the parts of 'olere' (to smell)
hints.ordior, ordiri, orsus sum, —: These are the parts of 'ordiri' (to begin) — a deponent verb

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'pater' — the -em ending marks accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'patre' — 'patrem' with -em is accusative
hints.dative: Dative would be 'patri' — 'patrem' is accusative, direct object of 'odit'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'patris' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'patris' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'patris' ends in -is

translation: If anyone comes to me and does not hate his father
